draft: Before you answer, do what a code reviewer does: read the vulnerable login template, substitute the attacker's input by hand, and simplify the resulting SQL on paper. The graded answers should just be reading off that work.

fields.label: What is the root cause of SQL injection?
options: User input is concatenated straight into the query string, so the database can't tell the developer's SQL from the attacker's input and parses all of it as code
SQL is an inherently insecure language that should be avoided
A bug in the database engine lets attackers run their own commands
The database's password storage is too weak to protect credentials

fields.label: A login runs WHERE username = '<input>' AND password = '<input>'. Which value, typed into the username box, logs an attacker in as the first user without knowing any password?
Why does the payload ' OR '1'='1' -- log the attacker in, but a bare ' OR '1'='1 (no --) does not?
options: The -- comments out the leftover AND password = '...' so only the always-true OR survives; without it, AND (which binds tighter than OR) still enforces the password and the stray quote breaks the syntax
They are identical; the -- is only a stylistic choice
The -- is needed because OR is not valid inside a WHERE clause
The -- deletes the users table before the password is checked

fields.label: Which of these actually REMOVES the vulnerability? Select every option that genuinely works — and only those.
options: Rewrite it as a parameterized query (prepared statement) with ? placeholders and pass the input as a separate value
Strip the word UNION out of the input before running it
Escape the quote characters in the input
Reject any input that contains two dashes
